Acylation of Enolates: The Claisen and Related Condensations
Section 21.4
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Claisen Condensation
The aldol reaction is a nucleophilic addition to C=O ; enolates can also engage in nucleophilic substitution
In the Claisen condensation , two equivalents of ester react to form a β - keto ester via substitution
One nucleophilic ester molecule is deprotonated and reacts with another electrophilic ester molecule
Why is this process thermodynamically favorable?

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation
Step 1: deprotonation by base to form an enolate
Step 2: nucleophilic addition to C=O

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation-1
Step 3: elimination of alkoxide
All steps have been reversible to this point (!)
What drives this reaction forward?

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation-2
Step 3: elimination of alkoxide
All steps have been reversible to this point (!)
What drives this reaction forward? An acid-base process

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation-3
Step 4: irreversible deprotonation of β - keto ester
Acid is added in workup to isolate the β - keto ester

Riffs on the Claisen Theme
The intramolecular Claisen condensation is called the Dieckmann condensation
Mixed Claisen condensations involve a non- enolizable electrophilic ester and a different nucleophilic ester
Ketones can be used as the source of the enolate rather than esters; β - keto ketones ( β - dicarbonyls ) are the products

ALKYLATION OF ENOLATES: THE ACETOACETIC ESTER AND MALONIC ESTER SYNTHESES
Section 21.5

Problems with Enolate Alkylation
Enolates of simple ketones and aldehydes undergo alkylation only with 1 degree and 2 degrees alkyl halides
Mixtures of different α -alkylated products can result, even from pre-formed enolates
Polyalkylation can occur
Aldol addition is an important competitive side reaction

β - dicarbonyl compounds are alkylated regioselectively at the position between the carbonyl groups
Two electron-withdrawing groups influence the central hydrogens, increasing their acidity
The conjugate base is stabilized by charge delocalization

Acetoacetic and Malonic Ester Syntheses
Acetoacetic and malonic esters contain highly acidic C–H positioned between two carbonyl groups
The ester can be removed in a later step via hydrolysis and decarboxylation
The three step method of alkylation - hydrolysis - decarboxylation enables regioselective monoalkylation of enolates

Acetoacetic and Malonic Ester Syntheses-1
Acetoacetic and malonic esters contain highly acidic C–H positioned between two carbonyl groups
The ester can be removed in a later step via hydrolysis and decarboxylation
The three step method of alkylation - hydrolysis - decarboxylation enables regioselective monoalkylation of enolates

Applications
Two successive alkylations of the ester lead to dialkylated products
α , ω - dihalides react twice to give cycloalkyl acids

Keto -enol Equilibrium
Carbonyls and enols are tautomers —constitutional isomers differing in the position of hydrogen
Keto-enol tautomerization is an important equilibrium for carbonyl compounds
K eq for keto-enol equilibrium can vary widely
For simple aldehydes and ketones, the keto form dominates
When the enol form is particularly stabilized by conjugation, the enol form dominates
The mechanism of tautomerization involves sequential protonation and deprotonation (or vice versa ), not concerted intramolecular proton transfer!

Keto -enol Equilibrium-1
Carbonyls and enols are tautomers —constitutional isomers differing in the position of hydrogen
Keto-enol tautomerization is an important equilibrium for carbonyl compounds
K eq for keto-enol equilibrium can vary widely

Mechanism of Tautomerization
Mechanism involves sequential protonation and deprotonation (or vice versa ), not concerted intramolecular proton transfer!
Under acid catalysis, protonation at O occurs first, followed by deprotonation at C

Mechanism of Tautomerization-1
Mechanism involves sequential protonation and deprotonation (or vice versa ), not concerted intramolecular proton transfer!
Under base catalysis, deprotonation at C occurs first (forming an enolate!), followed by deprotonation at O
